did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a multi-model deep neural network approach for skin lesion classification. Specifically, it employs pre-trained ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 models for deep feature extraction from dermoscopic images, followed by Kurtosis controlled PCA (KcPCA) for feature selection and fusion, and finally classifies the features using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel. This was applied to datasets including HAM10000, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017 for melanoma and skin cancer diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel automated system for skin lesion classification using a multi-model deep neural network (ResNet-50 and ResNet-101) and Kurtosis controlled PCA (KcPCA). It details specific methods including image preprocessing, feature extraction, and SVM classification, and reports new empirical results with accuracy metrics (89.8%, 95.60%, 90.20%) on three datasets (HAM10000, ISBI 2016, ISBI 2017). The structure includes Abstract, Introduction, Methodology, Results, and Discussion, characteristic of an original research study rather than a review.

title: Multi-Model Deep Neural Network based Features Extraction and Optimal Selection Approach for Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated computer-aided diagnosis system for classifying skin lesions, specifically targeting melanoma and other skin cancers. The methodology employs a multi-model deep neural network approach using pre-trained ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 architectures for feature extraction from dermoscopic images. To optimize feature selection, the authors introduce Kurtosis controlled PCA (KcPCA) to select the top 70% of features, which are then fused using a maximal probability approach. These fused features are classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel. The system was evaluated on three public datasets: HAM10000, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017. The proposed method achieved classification accuracies of 89.8%, 95.60%, and 90.20% on the respective datasets, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing techniques.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in HAM10000 dataset (class 'mel') and ISBI 2016/2017 datasets (malignant category).
Used as a category in ISBI 2016 (1006 images) and ISBI 2017 (2233 images) datasets, distinct from malignant cases.
Used as a category in ISBI 2016 (273 images) and ISBI 2017 (517 images) datasets, distinct from benign cases.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, nevi, vascular skin, actinic keratoses) or binary classes (malignant vs. benign)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class and binary classification of skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The system extracts deep features from ResNet-50 and ResNet-101, selects optimal features via KcPCA, and classifies them using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with an RBF kernel. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (7 classes in HAM10000, 2 classes in ISBI datasets) rather than continuous values. Performance is measured by accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic dataset consisting of RGB images used for skin lesion classification.

use.training: Training and testing samples were separately provided; a 70:30 ratio was not explicitly defined for this dataset as samples were separate, but K-fold cross-validation (K=10) was used for validation.

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic dataset containing RGB images used for skin lesion classification.

use.training: Training and testing samples were separately provided; K-fold cross-validation (K=10) was used for validation.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic dataset involving 10,000 images of six different classes (plus one additional class mentioned in text as 7 categories total in results section, though list has 6, text says 'seven different categories' in results section but lists 6 in dataset description section. The abstract and results mention 7 categories in the context of the challenge, but the specific list provided in Section III has 6. However, the text explicitly states 'seven different categories' in the results section for HAM10000. The classes listed in Section III are: Actinic Keratoses (akiec), basal cell carcinoma (bcc), benign keratosis (bkl), Dermatofibroma (df), Nevi (nv), melanoma (mel), and Vascular skin (vasc). This is 7 classes.

use.training: A training and testing ratio of 70:30 was set.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate testing classification results for all three datasets (HAM10000, ISBI 2016, and ISBI 2017).
To compute accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, AUC, and FP rate for each class and compare performance across different SVM kernels.
To select the top 70% of features based on higher scores of principal component analysis and kurtosis, serving as a feature selection method rather than a statistical hypothesis test.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, AUC, and FP rate, with comparisons made based on these metric values across different SVM kernels and datasets.

ISBI 2016.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, AUC, FP Rate
ISBI 2016.notes: The RBF kernel outperformed other kernels. The worst accuracy was 80.5% with the linear kernel. Results validated via 10-fold cross-validation.

ISBI 2017.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, AUC, FP Rate
ISBI 2017.notes: The RBF kernel achieved the best accuracy of 95.6%. The worst accuracy was 89.5% with the linear kernel. Results validated via 10-fold cross-validation.

HAM10000.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Precision, AUC, FP Rate
HAM10000.notes: The best accuracy of 89.8% was achieved with the RBF kernel. The worst accuracy of 85.2% was obtained with quadratic and FGSVM kernels. A 70:30 training/testing ratio was used. Results validated via 10-fold cross-validation.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-model Deep Neural Network (ResNet-50 and ResNet-101) combined with Kurtosis controlled PCA (KcPCA) and Support Vector Machine (SVM)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 models were utilized for feature extraction.

feature_selection.selection_criteria: Top 70% of features based on higher score of principal component analysis and kurtosis

training_details.hardware: Personal Computer Core i7, 16GB RAM, 8GB Graphics card
training_details.data_split: 70:30 training/testing ratio for HAM10000; separate training/testing samples for ISBI datasets

classification_or_output_head.output: Class labels (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Nevi, Vascular Skin, Actinic Keratoses for HAM10000; Malignant/Benign for ISBI datasets)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: SVM with Radial Basis Function (RBF) Kernel
preprocessing_techniques: Hybrid preprocessing technique for contrast enhancement and lesion highlighting
Fusion of complement operation and dehaze reduction
Dark channel prior (DCP) approach for haze reduction and visibility enhancement
LAB color space transformation to highlight the lesion region
conclusions: The proposed automated skin lesion classification system, utilizing pre-trained ResNet-50 and ResNet-101 for deep feature extraction and Kurtosis controlled PCA (KcPCA) for optimal feature selection, achieved high classification accuracy on three public datasets.
The system achieved an accuracy of 89.8% on the HAM10000 dataset, 95.60% on the ISBI 2017 dataset, and 90.20% on the ISBI 2016 dataset.
The Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel outperformed other tested kernels (Linear, Quadratic, Cubic, FGSVM, MGSVM, and CGSVM) across all three datasets.

Preprocessing steps, including hybrid contrast enhancement and dark channel prior haze reduction, are essential for highlighting lesion regions and improving feature extraction quality, particularly for complex images with noise and artifacts.
The use of KcPCA to select the top 70% of features based on kurtosis and PCA scores effectively reduced redundancy and improved classification accuracy compared to using all extracted features.
The proposed method demonstrates reliable performance compared to existing techniques, addressing the challenge of limited accuracy in manual diagnosis and existing computerized methods.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on public datasets (HAM10000, ISBI 2016, ISBI 2017) and lacks external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
The HAM10000 dataset is known to have significant class imbalance and inter-observer variability in ground truth labels, which may bias the reported accuracy of 89.8%.

The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the accuracy metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance of the performance improvements over baseline methods.
The feature selection method (KcPCA) selects the top 70% of features, but the specific impact of this arbitrary threshold on model robustness is not analyzed.

The preprocessing steps (hybrid contrast enhancement, dark channel prior) are applied uniformly; however, the paper does not discuss potential negative impacts on lesions with specific color profiles or artifacts that might be over-corrected.

The comparison with existing techniques in Table IV is limited to a small number of recent studies (2016-2018) and does not include state-of-the-art deep learning models (e.g., Inception, EfficientNet) that were available or emerging at the time of publication.
The hardware specifications (Core i7, 16GB RAM) suggest the model may not be easily deployable on resource-constrained edge devices or mobile dermatology applications without optimization.

The paper does not address potential data leakage risks regarding the preprocessing and feature extraction steps if they were not strictly separated between training and testing folds during cross-validation.
